video_title: Real Conversations | Rising Geostrategic Risk: The New Rules for Global Markets w/ John O’Connor
gist: Former JP Morgan alternatives head and Department of War special government employee John O'Connor argues the rise of China has ended the frictionless-globalization era, that geostrategic risk is mispriced at zero across the Street, and that his 10-factor Geostrategic Risk Rating (behind ETF ticker CRTC) quantifies each company's China entanglement. He frames a Quad 4 macro setup into late 2026/early 2027 driven by the November rare-earth waiver expiration and the Trump-Xi meeting.

insights.claim: John O'Connor believes the end-of-history, frictionless-globalization thesis (free movement of people, goods, ideas and capital) is over, and that the free movement of all four will now become less free, manifesting in rising trade barriers and a massive increase in defense spending.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Positions portfolios for de-globalization: long defense/reshoring, wary of asset prices that assume frictionless global trade and cheap capital.
insights.quote: we're bullish chaos. we're we're long chaos that conflict in all of its forms at all of its levels of aggreg aggregation will go up

insights.claim: O'Connor argues Trump is a symptom of, and response to, underlying macro phenomena (chiefly the disruptive rise of China) rather than the cause, so investors misread the shift by attributing it to one administration.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: The de-globalization regime persists regardless of US electoral outcomes, so it should be treated as a structural, multi-cycle factor, not a political trade.
insights.quote: Trump is really a symptom in response to phenomena more than he is a response in a phenomena himself

insights.claim: O'Connor frames the current moment as analogous to the 15th-century printing-press era leading into 200 years of war and the Peace of Westphalia, arguing the social contract and the role of personal agency must be rewritten.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Signals a multi-decade, non-linear transition — investors should expect elevated forecast volatility across growth, inflation and policy rather than mean reversion to prior norms.
insights.quote: I think we are in a period of that magnitude where the very social contract has to get rewritten

insights.claim: O'Connor contends nation-state behavior is no longer dictated by economic optimization but by power, meaning the Davos/optimization worldview will consistently misforecast outcomes like Russia's invasion of Ukraine.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Models that assume rational economic optimization by states will be systematically wrong; risk premia should embed power-driven, non-economic actions.
insights.quote: economic optimization is no longer dictating nation state behavior. Power is.

insights.claim: O'Connor says the PRC has deliberately made the US and West asymmetrically dependent on China (citing the wipeout of 13 of 16 global wireless equipment firms, leaving Nokia/Ericsson vs. ZTE/Huawei/Xiaomi) and is now repeating the pattern in AI.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Sectors where China can achieve scale dominance (telecom, now AI) face structural pricing and share erosion for Western incumbents.
insights.quote: they've got ZTE and Huawei and Xiaomi and whatnot. And we're about to go through it in AI

insights.claim: O'Connor argues buyers will choose cheap Chinese AI capability — roughly 80% of the capability at 30% of the cost — undercutting the Wall Street narrative that AI leadership is safely American because the large-cap names are US-listed.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Challenges premium valuations on US AI/semiconductor leaders; a low-cost Chinese alternative pressures the demand and margin assumptions embedded in those stocks.
insights.quote: if I can buy 80% of the capability at 30% of the cost I'm going to buy the cheap thing

insights.claim: O'Connor and McCullough cite Hedgeye tech analyst Felix Wong as holding a China-aligned, more bearish view of US AI positioning than the Wall Street consensus, and note the DeepSeek shock as a recurring, accelerating pattern on the trading desk.
insights.speaker: guest and host
insights.so_what: An in-house China-native analyst dissenting from consensus is a signal to stress-test US AI leadership assumptions against Chinese capability shocks.
insights.quote: The the the day the deep sea hit the first time was incredible

insights.claim: O'Connor says he exited several billion dollars of Asia exposure to zero over a couple of years after the 2008 Beijing Olympics opening ceremony (Admiral Zheng He fleet imagery) and Xi Jinping's 2012 rise, which he read as signaling a Maoist-Leninist turn.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: A practitioner example of using geopolitical/cultural signaling to time a full exit from a market well before consensus recognized the risk.
insights.quote: we at that point uh went from several billion dollars exposed to Asia to zero over the next couple of years

insights.claim: O'Connor recommends investors read China's four foundational 'global initiatives' documents (security, prosperity/development, well-being, and global governance) directly in translation as the explicit PRC roadmap, noting the governance initiative amounts to China stating it intends to run the global system.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Primary-source PRC strategy documents are a free, actionable roadmap for anticipating Chinese policy moves rather than relying on Western commentary.
insights.quote: they were four foundational documents which lay out the entire People's Republic of China plan. And they are the four global initiatives

insights.claim: O'Connor flags that China's rare-earth-element and permanent-magnet export waivers expire in November 2026, and cites an IEA study warning that non-extension could be as much as a $6.5 trillion GDP event to the downside.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: A concrete Q4 2026 catalyst with tail-risk magnitude — monitor waiver-extension news as a binary macro/supply-chain event.
insights.quote: we could see as much as a $6.5 trillion dollar GDP event the wrong way

insights.claim: O'Connor says China 'weaponized the periodic table,' controlling tiny critical-mineral markets with outsized impact, and cites tungsten (no tungsten, no machine tools) as a choke point for industrial and defense production.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Watch niche mineral prices (tungsten, indium, styrene, etc.) as leading indicators of supply-chain stress before equities reprice.
insights.quote: China weaponized the periodic table

insights.claim: O'Connor highlights US aluminum scarcity: the US produces under 1 million metric tons of aluminum vs. China's 80 million, while requiring ~2 million tons for military, ~2 million for the defense industrial base, and ~2 million for the open economy — a structural 5-million-ton shortfall.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Points to reshoring/onshoring demand for domestic aluminum and alumina producers as market access becomes treated as a national asset.
insights.quote: here in the United States, we produce less than 1 million metric tons of aluminum. China produces 80 million metric tons

insights.claim: O'Connor notes President Trump issued an executive order requiring the US defense industrial base to be more rigorous about materials access and supply chains — 'late to need, but it's here.'
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Signals accelerating US industrial policy and government-led onshoring demand for critical materials and defense suppliers.
insights.quote: just yesterday, President Trump put out another executive order basically requiring the defense industrial base to be more rigorous about its access to materials

insights.claim: O'Connor frames the setup as Quad 4 — growth and inflation decelerating together (stagflation into it) with corporate profits falling fast — landing potentially in Q1 2027, just after an expected September 2026 Trump-Xi meeting and the November rare-earth catalyst.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Aligns geopolitics with Hedgeye's Quad framework: defensive positioning into a growth-and-inflation-down regime with profit deceleration.
insights.quote: quad stagflation then quad four where you have both growth and inflation going down at the same time

insights.claim: O'Connor argues US equity valuations are stretched into the top quadrant — price-to-book of 4-5x, price-to-cash-flow of 15x, and forward earnings of 25x — leaving little margin for error going into a Quad 4 deceleration.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Elevated multiples plus decelerating profits imply asymmetric downside; a reason to trim broad index exposure into the catalysts.
insights.quote: I'm looking at price to book of four to five times and price to cash flow of 15 times and forward earnings of 25. I better be right now

insights.claim: O'Connor invokes Graham Allison's Thucydides Trap — in 13 of 16 historical cases a rising power confronting an incumbent went to war — to argue current great-power transitions will not be smooth, and warns war often starts by accident and miscalculation.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Assigns a non-trivial base rate to US-China conflict escalation, arguing for a persistent geostrategic risk premium in asset prices.
insights.quote: Graham Allison looked at the history of 16 times where rising power confronted an incumbent power in 13 of the 16 times they go to war

insights.claim: O'Connor argues the November rare-earth negotiation gives China leverage to tip the US into recession, which would hurt Republican midterm prospects, so a lot of money is on the table in Q4 2026 with a wide range of outcomes.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Frames Q4 2026 as a high-variance policy-negotiation window with direct implications for US growth and risk assets.
insights.quote: they have a lot of influence over tipping the United States into a recession, which would probably not be good for Republican midterm election prospects

insights.claim: O'Connor says Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent must sell roughly $100 billion of bonds a month just to refinance ('meet his quota') plus another ~$100 billion for the incremental funding need, calling losing control of the yield curve the biggest unknown-unknown.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Massive supply plus absent bond vigilantes elevates the risk of a disorderly long-end repricing — a key tail risk to watch.
insights.quote: he's got to sell what a hundred billion dollars of bonds a month just to meet his quota, just to refi

insights.claim: O'Connor suggests oil has become 'the new governor of the yield curve' and the centerpiece of chaos, noting oil is currently trading around 60 volatility versus prior 120 — higher than the VIX reached even in 2008.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Treat oil vol as a leading input to bond yields and macro chaos; a re-acceleration in oil volatility would pressure rates.
insights.quote: if oil is the new governor of the ill curve and oil is the, you know, the centerpiece of chaos

insights.claim: O'Connor is 'hopeful but guarded' on incoming Fed chair Kevin Warsh, noting Warsh is bringing in external advisers (including people from Andreessen Horowitz) but signaling he is not Janet Yellen and unlikely to pursue yield-curve control.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: A Warsh Fed less inclined to yield-curve control raises the risk the market, not the Fed, sets the long end — bearish duration signal.
insights.quote: Kevin's like guess what I'm not Janet Yellen

insights.claim: O'Connor predicts that sometime in the next presidential administration the US will face a bond-market forcing event akin to 2011's poor long-bond auctions, requiring a Simpson-Bowles-style dollar-for-dollar fix that raises revenue and cuts spending from the current ~55% spending / 45% revenue imbalance.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Anticipate a future fiscal-discipline shock driven by bond-market pressure, with implications for rates and risk assets.
insights.quote: we cannot consume more than we per produce in perpetuity. So it's real simple. We're going to consume less and produce more

insights.claim: O'Connor built a 10-factor Geostrategic Risk Rating (GRR) scoring each factor 0-3, using China entanglement — share of revenue, manufacturing, supply chain and raw materials tied to the PRC — as the proxy for severity of impact, developed with help from government sources.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: A rules-based, quantifiable screen for China exposure that can be applied to individual stocks and over 3,000 ETFs to flag mispriced entanglement risk.
insights.quote: the proxy for that is entanglement... So we came up with this 10 factor model

insights.claim: O'Connor says he asked former JP Morgan colleagues what geostrategic risk premium sits in their CAPM / security market line and was told it is zero — which he argues cannot be correct, likening it to the pre-crisis compression of German, Greek, Italian and Portuguese yields to the same level.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: If the Street prices geostrategic risk at zero, entangled names are systematically overvalued — the core edge behind the GRR/CRTC thesis.
insights.quote: what's the geopolitical or geostrategic risk premium in your capital asset pricing model... yeah it's zero what's your point

insights.claim: O'Connor says the GRR framework screened out Tesla because its single largest production facility is in China on land owned by and licensed from the Chinese government, and screened out Micron by just 0.01 (rounding down) — missing the rally as an accepted cost of a rules-based system.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Illustrates how the entanglement screen flags names with concentrated China production dependency that consensus valuations ignore.
insights.quote: you screen out Tesla because of the dependencies. Their single largest production facility... is in China... on land owned by the Chinese government, operated on a license from the Chinese government

insights.claim: O'Connor argues Apple carries unpriced China risk, recounting a claim that when Apple leadership cited employing ~7 million people in China as leverage, Chinese officials replied they could change the sign on the factory and make Xiaomi phones tomorrow; and notes fires in Apple's early India factories plus China recalling its engineers from India.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Frames Apple as exposed to scenario-driven 'couple of bad days' from China dependency that the market has not yet priced.
insights.quote: at the moment you employ seven million we can change the sign on your factory tomorrow

insights.claim: O'Connor describes the geostrategically-informed investment analysis being embraced by Deutsche Asset Management, UBS and Brookfield, and packaged in an ETF with ticker CRTC, positioning it as what ESG should have been — rules-based rather than 'magic.'
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Identifies CRTC as an investable, rules-based geostrategic-risk product; institutional adoption is an emerging distribution signal.
insights.quote: this whole field of geo strategically geopolitically informed um investment analysis is what ESG should have been instead of magic

insights.claim: O'Connor argues globalization rewarded companies for thinning supply chains and running near-zero working capital, making Western firms brittle while the PRC optimized for control and power — creating today's dependency problem.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Resilience (inventory buffers, redundant supply) becomes a re-rated corporate virtue; brittle, thin-margin optimizers face rising re-engineering costs.
insights.quote: we rewarded companies for thinning out supply chains for running on zero working capital... became incredibly thin, brittle enterprises

insights.claim: O'Connor cites automakers halting production lines over shortages of cheap 30-nanometer legacy analog chips (side-mirror motor controllers), where the fix was simply holding far more inventory — from ~10 minutes of inventory to ~20 days.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Structural shift from just-in-time to just-in-case inventory raises working-capital needs across industrials — a margin and cash-flow headwind.
insights.quote: Instead of 10 minutes of inventory in system, I need 20 days. Get over it.

insights.claim: O'Connor says US reshoring of manufacturing, with the Department of War effectively leading industrial policy, has already begun and will bring production, investment and employment back onshore, gradually addressing wealth and income imbalances.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Supports a multi-year onshoring/industrial-policy investment theme led by defense-adjacent government demand.
insights.quote: the reshoring of manufacturing and the Department of War stepping up and essentially leading industrial policy is the beginning of bringing production back on shore

insights.claim: O'Connor argues China pursues electrification and now has 12 gigawatt-scale-and-above nuclear reactors underway versus roughly half of one in the US, aiming to give its industrial base near-free energy as an export-model advantage while competitors like Mercedes-Benz pay ~26 cents per kilowatt-hour.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: China's cheap-power industrial strategy is a durable cost-competitiveness threat to Western manufacturers and a tailwind for nuclear/energy buildout themes.
insights.quote: they currently have 12 gigawatt scale and above nuclear reactors underway... We have a half of one.

insights.claim: O'Connor argues the key emerging value of information is verifiability, warning that debased information turns communication into defending a predetermined point rather than arriving at an analysis, making disciplined Bayesian scenario-testing more valuable than ever.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Edge accrues to process-driven, assumption-challenging analysts as the cost of information collapses and misinformation rises.
insights.quote: communication becomes using uh using your information um to defend a point, not arrive at an analysis

insights.claim: O'Connor states that well over 90% of daily market buys and sells are machine orders within one standard deviation, so 'the noise has become the signal' and most price action reflects relative-value machine trading rather than price-to-value.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: In a passive/machine-dominated market, fundamental geostrategic dislocations stay unpriced longer — but such regimes tend to reverse, rewarding signal-based positioning.
insights.quote: Well over 90% of all buys and sells in the market are machine orders every day. There's not people making considered decisions. So, the noise has become the signal.

insights.claim: O'Connor observes the US and China both carry debt of roughly three times GDP and jointly depend on global savings pools, calling them 'two drunks leaning against each other in a bar' — making global asset allocators the most important constituency.
insights.speaker: John O'Connor
insights.so_what: Both superpowers are hostage to global capital flows; a shift in allocator sentiment is a shared systemic vulnerability to monitor.
insights.quote: we're both levered up to three times GDP... we're sort of like two drunks leaning against each other in a bar
